Project_2_SMART_Question_3

Maurice Goode

2026-04-21

Project 2 - SMART Question 3

Step 1: Data Preparation

# Load Libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(rpart)          # Decision tree (Class 12)
library(rpart.plot)     # Tree visualization
## Warning: package 'rpart.plot' was built under R version 4.5.3
library(randomForest)   # Bagging & random forest (Class 12)
## Warning: package 'randomForest' was built under R version 4.5.3
## randomForest 4.7-1.2
## Type rfNews() to see new features/changes/bug fixes.
## 
## Attaching package: 'randomForest'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     combine
## The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':
## 
##     margin
library(pROC)           # ROC-AUC (Class 11)
## Warning: package 'pROC' was built under R version 4.5.3
## Type 'citation("pROC")' for a citation.
## 
## Attaching package: 'pROC'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     cov, smooth, var
library(caret)          # Confusion matrix helper
## Warning: package 'caret' was built under R version 4.5.3
## Loading required package: lattice
library(pdp)            
## Warning: package 'pdp' was built under R version 4.5.3
library(gridExtra)      
## 
## Attaching package: 'gridExtra'
## The following object is masked from 'package:randomForest':
## 
##     combine
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     combine
# Load & Prepare Data
df <- read.csv("credit_risk_dataset.csv")

# Convert categorical variables to factors (as done in Project 1)
df$person_home_ownership      <- as.factor(df$person_home_ownership)
df$loan_intent                <- as.factor(df$loan_intent)
df$loan_grade                 <- as.factor(df$loan_grade)
df$cb_person_default_on_file  <- as.factor(df$cb_person_default_on_file)
df$loan_status                <- as.factor(df$loan_status)   # 0=non-default, 1=default

df <- na.omit(df)

cat("Rows after NA removal:", nrow(df), "\n")   # expect ~28,638
## Rows after NA removal: 28638
# Check class balance (important for classification — Class 11)
table(df$loan_status)
## 
##     0     1 
## 22435  6203
prop.table(table(df$loan_status))
## 
##         0         1 
## 0.7833997 0.2166003
# Quick look at interest rate vs default
ggplot(df, aes(x = loan_status, y = loan_int_rate, fill = loan_status)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#3B8BD4", "#D85A30"),
                    labels = c("Non-default", "Default")) +
  labs(
    title = "Loan Interest Rate by Default Status",
    subtitle = "Visual check: does interest rate differ between groups?",
    x = "Loan Status (0 = Non-default, 1 = Default)",
    y = "Interest Rate (%)",
    fill = "Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

A quick EDA was performed to convert the appropriate fields to be factors. Next we removed rows that were missing values. After that we checked the overall count of non-default borrowers vs. default borrowers. An extraordinary number of 78% are non-default borrowers. This is an imbalance that could skew the training data later in our analysis. A boxplot was produced to examine the initial interest rates based on default status. As expected, non-default borrowers had a lower median interest rate vs. default borrowers.

Step 2: Train/Test Split (80/20)

# (Class 12: randomly assign observations — do not take first x%)
set.seed(42)   # Reproducibility

n         <- nrow(df)
train_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.80 * n), replace = FALSE)

train <- df[ train_idx, ]
test  <- df[-train_idx, ]

cat("Training rows:", nrow(train), "\n")
## Training rows: 22910
cat("Test rows    :", nrow(test),  "\n")
## Test rows    : 5728
cat("Default rate (train):", round(mean(train$loan_status == 1), 3), "\n")
## Default rate (train): 0.216
cat("Default rate (test) :", round(mean(test$loan_status  == 1), 3), "\n")
## Default rate (test) : 0.221

For our training and test sets we used the standard 80/20 split. To verify the split is representative, we compared the default rate in the training set (21.6%) to the test set (22.1%)

Step 3: Model 1- Logistic Regression (MLR with logit link)

# MODEL 1: LOGISTIC REGRESSION (GLM — Class 10 & 11)
# Dependent variable: loan_status (binary)
# Key predictor of interest: loan_int_rate
# Controls: borrower & loan characteristics

logit_model <- glm(
  loan_status ~ loan_int_rate +         # Key predictor of interest
    person_age +
    person_income +
    person_home_ownership +
    person_emp_length +
    loan_amnt +
    loan_intent +
    loan_grade +
    loan_percent_income +
    cb_person_default_on_file +
    cb_person_cred_hist_length,
  data   = train,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")     # logit link for binary outcome
)

# Full summary: coefficients, p-values, AIC (Class 11)
summary(logit_model)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = loan_status ~ loan_int_rate + person_age + person_income + 
##     person_home_ownership + person_emp_length + loan_amnt + loan_intent + 
##     loan_grade + loan_percent_income + cb_person_default_on_file + 
##     cb_person_cred_hist_length, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
##     data = train)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)                -4.086e+00  2.233e-01 -18.296  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_int_rate               8.439e-02  2.042e-02   4.133 3.58e-05 ***
## person_age                 -1.617e-02  7.022e-03  -2.304  0.02125 *  
## person_income               9.749e-07  3.233e-07   3.015  0.00257 ** 
## person_home_ownershipOTHER  5.092e-01  3.319e-01   1.534  0.12492    
## person_home_ownershipOWN   -1.973e+00  1.327e-01 -14.867  < 2e-16 ***
## person_home_ownershipRENT   8.353e-01  4.817e-02  17.341  < 2e-16 ***
## person_emp_length          -1.517e-02  5.626e-03  -2.696  0.00702 ** 
## loan_amnt                  -1.011e-04  4.731e-06 -21.371  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_intentEDUCATION       -8.855e-01  6.819e-02 -12.986  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_intentHOMEIMPROVEMENT  4.164e-02  7.625e-02   0.546  0.58503    
## loan_intentMEDICAL         -1.694e-01  6.490e-02  -2.610  0.00906 ** 
## loan_intentPERSONAL        -6.874e-01  7.017e-02  -9.796  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_intentVENTURE         -1.174e+00  7.514e-02 -15.631  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeB                 1.160e-01  9.350e-02   1.241  0.21461    
## loan_gradeC                 3.236e-01  1.400e-01   2.312  0.02077 *  
## loan_gradeD                 2.413e+00  1.760e-01  13.714  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeE                 2.507e+00  2.221e-01  11.286  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeF                 2.762e+00  3.053e-01   9.047  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeG                 6.229e+00  1.074e+00   5.798 6.71e-09 ***
## loan_percent_income         1.333e+01  2.798e-01  47.656  < 2e-16 ***
## cb_person_default_on_fileY -1.369e-03  5.941e-02  -0.023  0.98162    
## cb_person_cred_hist_length  1.913e-02  1.063e-02   1.800  0.07182 .  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 23884  on 22909  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 15209  on 22887  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 15255
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
# Coefficient Interpretation (Class 10)
# Extract odds ratios for easier interpretation
odds_ratios <- exp(coef(logit_model))
ci          <- exp(confint(logit_model))
## Waiting for profiling to be done...
logit_results <- data.frame(
  OddsRatio = round(odds_ratios, 4),
  CI_Lower  = round(ci[, 1], 4),
  CI_Upper  = round(ci[, 2], 4)
)
print(logit_results)
##                              OddsRatio    CI_Lower     CI_Upper
## (Intercept)                     0.0168      0.0108       0.0260
## loan_int_rate                   1.0881      1.0454       1.1325
## person_age                      0.9840      0.9705       0.9975
## person_income                   1.0000      1.0000       1.0000
## person_home_ownershipOTHER      1.6640      0.8536       3.1432
## person_home_ownershipOWN        0.1391      0.1066       0.1795
## person_home_ownershipRENT       2.3054      2.0983       2.5344
## person_emp_length               0.9849      0.9742       0.9959
## loan_amnt                       0.9999      0.9999       0.9999
## loan_intentEDUCATION            0.4125      0.3608       0.4714
## loan_intentHOMEIMPROVEMENT      1.0425      0.8976       1.2103
## loan_intentMEDICAL              0.8442      0.7434       0.9587
## loan_intentPERSONAL             0.5029      0.4382       0.5769
## loan_intentVENTURE              0.3090      0.2665       0.3578
## loan_gradeB                     1.1230      0.9351       1.3491
## loan_gradeC                     1.3821      1.0505       1.8184
## loan_gradeD                    11.1723      7.9175      15.7841
## loan_gradeE                    12.2642      7.9419      18.9706
## loan_gradeF                    15.8299      8.7173      28.8584
## loan_gradeG                   507.4689     91.8826    9572.6288
## loan_percent_income        617953.6085 357716.7644 1073359.5363
## cb_person_default_on_fileY      0.9986      0.8889       1.1220
## cb_person_cred_hist_length      1.0193      0.9983       1.0408
cat("\n--- Key Interpretation for loan_int_rate ---\n")
## 
## --- Key Interpretation for loan_int_rate ---
cat(sprintf(
  "Odds Ratio = %.4f: A 1%% increase in interest rate multiplies the\n  odds of default by %.4f, holding all else constant.\n",
  odds_ratios["loan_int_rate"], odds_ratios["loan_int_rate"]
))
## Odds Ratio = 1.0881: A 1% increase in interest rate multiplies the
##   odds of default by 1.0881, holding all else constant.
# McFadden R² (Class 11 pseudo-R²)
null_model   <- glm(loan_status ~ 1, data = train, family = binomial)
mcfadden_r2  <- 1 - (logLik(logit_model) / logLik(null_model))
cat(sprintf("\nMcFadden R² = %.4f\n", mcfadden_r2))
## 
## McFadden R² = 0.3632
# Predictions on Test Set
logit_probs  <- predict(logit_model, newdata = test, type = "response")
logit_preds  <- ifelse(logit_probs >= 0.5, 1, 0)
logit_preds  <- as.factor(logit_preds)

# Confusion Matrix (Class 11)
cm_logit <- confusionMatrix(logit_preds, test$loan_status, positive = "1")
print(cm_logit)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 4255  579
##          1  209  685
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.8624          
##                  95% CI : (0.8532, 0.8713)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7793          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.5531          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.5419          
##             Specificity : 0.9532          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.7662          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.8802          
##              Prevalence : 0.2207          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1196          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.1561          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.7476          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : 1               
## 
# ROC-AUC (Class 11)
roc_logit <- roc(response  = as.numeric(test$loan_status) - 1,
                 predictor = logit_probs)
## Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1
## Setting direction: controls < cases
plot(roc_logit,
     main = "ROC Curve — Logistic Regression",
     col  = "#3B8BD4",
     lwd  = 2)

cat(sprintf("\nLogistic Regression AUC = %.4f\n", auc(roc_logit)))
## 
## Logistic Regression AUC = 0.8593

To adequately answer this SMART Question, we used three models to analyze the data thoroughly We chose a logistic regression, a decision tree, and a random forest. Most of the columns were chosen as key predictors. Some variables that were not statistically significant are age, income, cb_person_default_on_file, and cb_person_cred_hist_length. Their p-values were above 0.5 Then we looked at the odds ratio. Variables with an odds ratio above 2 are: person_home_ownershipRENT, loan_grade_D, loan_grade_E, loan_grade_F, loan_grade_G, and loan_percent_income. None of the variables had a negative odds ratio.

In general, below average loan grades and the debt-to-income ratio are key factors for loan underwriting. What stood out to use though was how high the odds ratio was for loan_grade_G and loan_percent_income. Respectfully, for every 1% increase in those variables there is a 507% and 617953% chance that a borrower will default on the loan.

In regards to the interest rate, a 1% increase multiplies the odds of default by 1.0881, holding all else constant. The training data explains 36.3% of the variability in the model due to its McFadden R² value. The relationship is statistically significant at any conventional threshold.

Switching gears to the Confusion Matrix. This logistic model has a moderate sensitivity of 54% but a high specificity of 95%. Translating to a balance of actual values and false values from a sensitivity side. On the other hand, the model is highly specific which is not a great sign. Finally, the AUC had a score of 85.93% which is good. However, one caveat that we noticed during EDA is that 78% of the borrowers are non-default. Additionally methodologies will be needed to draw a more concise conclusion.

Step 4: Model 2- Decision Tree (Single, Pruned)

# MODEL 2: CLASSIFICATION DECISION TREE (Class 12)
# rpart uses Gini impurity / entropy for splits
# Alpha (cp) controls pruning — use cross-validation to select
# Fit a full tree first
tree_full <- rpart(
  loan_status ~ loan_int_rate +
    person_age +
    person_income +
    person_home_ownership +
    person_emp_length +
    loan_amnt +
    loan_intent +
    loan_grade +
    loan_percent_income +
    cb_person_default_on_file +
    cb_person_cred_hist_length,
  data   = train,
  method = "class",                # classification tree (Class 12)
  control = rpart.control(cp = 0)  # start unpruned
)

# Cross-Validation to select optimal cp (alpha)
# (Class 12: use CV to tune the complexity parameter)
printcp(tree_full)    # Table of cp vs. cross-validated error
## 
## Classification tree:
## rpart(formula = loan_status ~ loan_int_rate + person_age + person_income + 
##     person_home_ownership + person_emp_length + loan_amnt + loan_intent + 
##     loan_grade + loan_percent_income + cb_person_default_on_file + 
##     cb_person_cred_hist_length, data = train, method = "class", 
##     control = rpart.control(cp = 0))
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
##  [1] cb_person_cred_hist_length cb_person_default_on_file 
##  [3] loan_amnt                  loan_grade                
##  [5] loan_int_rate              loan_intent               
##  [7] loan_percent_income        person_age                
##  [9] person_emp_length          person_home_ownership     
## [11] person_income             
## 
## Root node error: 4939/22910 = 0.21558
## 
## n= 22910 
## 
##            CP nsplit rel error  xerror      xstd
## 1  2.2089e-01      0   1.00000 1.00000 0.0126024
## 2  1.0974e-01      1   0.77911 0.77911 0.0114565
## 3  8.3013e-02      2   0.66937 0.66937 0.0107689
## 4  6.1956e-02      4   0.50334 0.50334 0.0095317
## 5  3.6445e-02      5   0.44138 0.44138 0.0089924
## 6  2.4296e-02      6   0.40494 0.40494 0.0086505
## 7  1.9235e-02      7   0.38064 0.38085 0.0084130
## 8  9.7186e-03      8   0.36141 0.36141 0.0082142
## 9  6.8840e-03      9   0.35169 0.35189 0.0081144
## 10 6.0741e-03     10   0.34481 0.34845 0.0080778
## 11 3.8469e-03     12   0.33266 0.33185 0.0078983
## 12 2.8346e-03     15   0.32112 0.32071 0.0077746
## 13 2.6321e-03     16   0.31828 0.31990 0.0077656
## 14 2.0247e-03     17   0.31565 0.31727 0.0077359
## 15 1.8222e-03     18   0.31363 0.31585 0.0077199
## 16 1.1642e-03     19   0.31180 0.31140 0.0076692
## 17 8.0988e-04     23   0.30715 0.31302 0.0076877
## 18 7.0865e-04     26   0.30472 0.31261 0.0076831
## 19 5.3992e-04     38   0.29419 0.31018 0.0076553
## 20 5.0618e-04     43   0.29115 0.31059 0.0076599
## 21 4.0494e-04     51   0.28629 0.31180 0.0076738
## 22 3.1495e-04     60   0.28265 0.31504 0.0077107
## 23 3.0371e-04     70   0.27921 0.31849 0.0077496
## 24 2.6996e-04     74   0.27799 0.31990 0.0077656
## 25 2.4746e-04     79   0.27657 0.32253 0.0077951
## 26 2.0247e-04     88   0.27435 0.33003 0.0078782
## 27 1.5748e-04    104   0.27091 0.33347 0.0079160
## 28 1.5185e-04    128   0.26564 0.33610 0.0079448
## 29 1.3498e-04    132   0.26503 0.33914 0.0079778
## 30 1.0124e-04    143   0.26220 0.34217 0.0080106
## 31 8.9987e-05    161   0.26038 0.34967 0.0080907
## 32 6.7490e-05    170   0.25957 0.35189 0.0081144
## 33 5.0618e-05    185   0.25855 0.35513 0.0081485
## 34 4.0494e-05    205   0.25754 0.35979 0.0081973
## 35 2.8924e-05    210   0.25734 0.36303 0.0082310
## 36 0.0000e+00    217   0.25714 0.36445 0.0082457
plotcp(tree_full)     # Visual: pick cp at the elbow/minimum

# Select the cp that minimizes cross-validated error
best_cp <- tree_full$cptable[which.min(tree_full$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
cat(sprintf("\nOptimal cp (alpha) = %.6f\n", best_cp))
## 
## Optimal cp (alpha) = 0.000540
# Prune the tree
tree_pruned <- prune(tree_full, cp = best_cp)

# Visualize the Pruned Tree
rpart.plot(
  tree_pruned,
  type   = 4,
  extra  = 104,
  fallen.leaves = TRUE,
  main   = "Pruned Classification Tree — Loan Default"
)
## Warning: labs do not fit even at cex 0.15, there may be some overplotting

# We isolate the branch starting at Node 2
tree_node_2 <- snip.rpart(tree_pruned, toss = 2)

# Now we plot the isolated branch
rpart.plot(
  tree_node_2,
  type  = 4,
  extra = 104,
  tweak = 1.0, 
  main  = "Zoomed View: Branch 2 Context"
)

# Variable Importance (Class 12)
var_imp_tree <- tree_pruned$variable.importance
sort(var_imp_tree, decreasing = TRUE)
##        loan_percent_income                 loan_grade 
##                1577.831619                1307.779986 
##      person_home_ownership              loan_int_rate 
##                1081.912742                 973.099853 
##              person_income                loan_intent 
##                 542.489978                 487.426278 
##          person_emp_length                  loan_amnt 
##                 268.566777                 200.695176 
##                 person_age  cb_person_default_on_file 
##                  71.841957                  14.712998 
## cb_person_cred_hist_length 
##                   7.451624
# Bar plot of variable importance
par(mar = c(12, 4, 4, 2))

barplot(
  sort(var_imp_tree, decreasing = TRUE),
  las  = 2,
  #cex.names = 0.6,
  col  = "#1D9E75",
  main = "Variable Importance — Decision Tree",
  ylab = "Importance"
)

# Predictions on Test Set
tree_probs <- predict(tree_pruned, newdata = test, type = "prob")[, 2]
tree_preds <- predict(tree_pruned, newdata = test, type = "class")

# Confusion Matrix (Class 11/12)
cm_tree <- confusionMatrix(tree_preds, test$loan_status, positive = "1")
print(cm_tree)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 4444  372
##          1   20  892
##                                          
##                Accuracy : 0.9316         
##                  95% CI : (0.9247, 0.938)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7793         
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16      
##                                          
##                   Kappa : 0.779          
##                                          
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16      
##                                          
##             Sensitivity : 0.7057         
##             Specificity : 0.9955         
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.9781         
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9228         
##              Prevalence : 0.2207         
##          Detection Rate : 0.1557         
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.1592         
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.8506         
##                                          
##        'Positive' Class : 1              
## 
# ROC-AUC
roc_tree <- roc(response  = as.numeric(test$loan_status) - 1,
                predictor = tree_probs)
## Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1
## Setting direction: controls < cases
plot(roc_tree,
     main = "ROC Curve — Decision Tree",
     col  = "#1D9E75",
     lwd  = 2)

cat(sprintf("\nDecision Tree AUC = %.4f\n", auc(roc_tree)))
## 
## Decision Tree AUC = 0.9032

Step 5: Model 3 - Random Forest

# Building the Random Forest based on the full predictor set 
# (Random Forest internally handles feature selection and interactions)
# Building the Random Forest using the 'train' dataset
set.seed(42)
rf_model <- randomForest(loan_status ~ ., 
                         data = train, 
                         ntree = 500, 
                         importance = TRUE)

# Visualizing which variables drive the model most
varImpPlot(rf_model, main = "Random Forest Variable Importance")

# Partial Dependence Plot (PDP) Analysis

# Required Libraries
library(randomForest)   
library(pdp)            
library(ggplot2)        
library(gridExtra)      
library(dplyr)          


# Univariate PDP — loan_int_rate only
set.seed(42)
pdp_int_rate <- partial(
  rf_model,
  pred.var  = "loan_int_rate",   # variable to profile
  prob      = TRUE,              # return class probabilities
  which.class = 2,               # class "1" = default
  train     = train,             # data used to fit the model
  grid.resolution = 50,          # number of grid points
  progress  = "text"             # show progress bar
)

# Inspect the raw PDP values
head(pdp_int_rate)
##   loan_int_rate      yhat
## 1      5.420000 0.1999278
## 2      5.768163 0.1996491
## 3      6.116327 0.1985952
## 4      6.464490 0.1989493
## 5      6.812653 0.1994340
## 6      7.160816 0.2000801
# Plot 1: Univariate PDP
p1 <- ggplot(pdp_int_rate, aes(x = loan_int_rate, y = yhat)) +
  geom_line(color = "#009E73", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_rug(
    data  = train,
    aes(x = loan_int_rate),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    alpha = 0.05,
    sides = "b"     
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Partial Dependence Plot — loan_int_rate",
    subtitle = "Marginal effect on P(Default) after averaging over all other predictors",
    x        = "Loan Interest Rate (%)",
    y        = "Predicted P(Default)",
    caption  = "Rug marks show the observed distribution of interest rates in the training set."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40"),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 9)
  )

print(p1)

# Interaction PDP — loan_int_rate × loan_grade
set.seed(42)
pdp_int_grade <- partial(
  rf_model,
  pred.var    = c("loan_int_rate", "loan_grade"),
  prob        = TRUE,
  which.class = 2,
  train       = train,
  grid.resolution = 30,
  progress    = "text"
)

# Plot 2: Interaction PDP faceted by loan_grade
p2 <- ggplot(pdp_int_grade,
             aes(x = loan_int_rate, y = yhat, color = loan_grade)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ loan_grade, nrow = 2) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    title    = "Partial Dependence: loan_int_rate × loan_grade",
    subtitle = "Does interest rate retain a gradient WITHIN each grade?",
    x        = "Loan Interest Rate (%)",
    y        = "Predicted P(Default)",
    color    = "Loan Grade"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"   # grade already shown as facet label
  )

print(p2)

# Correlation Check — interest rate vs. grade
train_check <- train %>%
  mutate(grade_num = as.numeric(loan_grade))   # A=1, B=2, … G=7

spearman_rho <- cor(
  train_check$loan_int_rate,
  train_check$grade_num,
  method = "spearman"
)

cat(sprintf(
  "\nSpearman correlation (loan_int_rate vs. loan_grade rank): rho = %.4f\n",
  spearman_rho
))
## 
## Spearman correlation (loan_int_rate vs. loan_grade rank): rho = 0.9473
# Boxplot to visualize the overlap
p3 <- ggplot(train, aes(x = loan_grade, y = loan_int_rate, fill = loan_grade)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.size = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn", direction = -1) +
  labs(
    title    = "Interest Rate Distribution by Loan Grade",
    subtitle = "High overlap across grades = evidence of multicollinearity",
    x        = "Loan Grade",
    y        = "Interest Rate (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

print(p3)

# Save all three plots to a single PNG
png("PDP_loan_int_rate_analysis.png", width = 14, height = 10,
    units = "in", res = 150)
grid.arrange(p1, p3, p2, ncol = 2,
             layout_matrix = rbind(c(1, 2), c(3, 3)))
dev.off()
## png 
##   2
cat("\nPlot saved to: PDP_loan_int_rate_analysis.png\n")
## 
## Plot saved to: PDP_loan_int_rate_analysis.png
# Key Numerical Summaries
cat("\n--- PDP Summary: loan_int_rate ---\n")
## 
## --- PDP Summary: loan_int_rate ---
cat(sprintf("  Min  P(Default) at rate = %.1f%%:  %.4f\n",
            pdp_int_rate$loan_int_rate[which.min(pdp_int_rate$yhat)],
            min(pdp_int_rate$yhat)))
##   Min  P(Default) at rate = 6.1%:  0.1986
cat(sprintf("  Max  P(Default) at rate = %.1f%%:  %.4f\n",
            pdp_int_rate$loan_int_rate[which.max(pdp_int_rate$yhat)],
            max(pdp_int_rate$yhat)))
##   Max  P(Default) at rate = 20.7%:  0.4943
cat(sprintf("  Total lift (max - min):             %.4f (%.1f pp)\n",
            diff(range(pdp_int_rate$yhat)),
            diff(range(pdp_int_rate$yhat)) * 100))
##   Total lift (max - min):             0.2957 (29.6 pp)
cat(sprintf("\nSpearman rho (int_rate vs. grade):    %.4f\n", spearman_rho))
## 
## Spearman rho (int_rate vs. grade):    0.9473
cat("  -> A rho near 1 confirms strong collinearity.\n")
##   -> A rho near 1 confirms strong collinearity.
cat("  -> Despite this, if the PDP shows a positive gradient,\n")
##   -> Despite this, if the PDP shows a positive gradient,
cat("     loan_int_rate still carries independent signal.\n\n")
##      loan_int_rate still carries independent signal.
# Generating probability predictions on the 'test' dataset
rf_probs <- predict(rf_model, test, type = "prob")[,2]
rf_preds <- ifelse(rf_probs > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Confusion Matrix for Random Forest
cm_rf <- confusionMatrix(as.factor(rf_preds), as.factor(test$loan_status), positive = "1")
print(cm_rf)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 4446  367
##          1   18  897
##                                          
##                Accuracy : 0.9328         
##                  95% CI : (0.926, 0.9391)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7793         
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16      
##                                          
##                   Kappa : 0.7831         
##                                          
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16      
##                                          
##             Sensitivity : 0.7097         
##             Specificity : 0.9960         
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.9803         
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9237         
##              Prevalence : 0.2207         
##          Detection Rate : 0.1566         
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.1597         
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.8528         
##                                          
##        'Positive' Class : 1              
##

Step 6 - Model Comparison & Answer to SMART Question

# ============================================================
# ROC CURVE COMPARISON
# ============================================================

# 1. Get probabilities for existing models on the test set
# (Assuming logit_model and pruned_tree were also trained on 'train')
logit_probs <- predict(logit_model, test, type = "response")
tree_probs  <- predict(tree_pruned, test, type = "prob")[,2]

# 2. Calculate ROC objects
roc_logit <- roc(test$loan_status, logit_probs)
## Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1
## Setting direction: controls < cases
roc_tree  <- roc(test$loan_status, tree_probs)
## Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1
## Setting direction: controls < cases
roc_rf    <- roc(test$loan_status, rf_probs)
## Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1
## Setting direction: controls < cases
# 3. Plot the combined ROC Curve
plot(roc_logit, col = "blue", main = "ROC Curve Comparison for Loan Default", lwd = 2)
plot(roc_tree, add = TRUE, col = "orange", lwd = 2)
plot(roc_rf, add = TRUE, col = "#009E73", lwd = 2)

# Add Legend
legend("bottomright", 
       legend = c(paste("Logistic Regression (AUC =", round(auc(roc_logit), 3), ")"),
                  paste("Pruned Decision Tree (AUC =", round(auc(roc_tree), 3), ")"),
                  paste("Random Forest (AUC =", round(auc(roc_rf), 3), ")")),
       col = c("blue", "orange", "#009E73"), 
       lwd = 2)

# ============================================================
# FINAL PERFORMANCE SUMMARY
# ============================================================
comparison_metrics <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Logistic Regression", "Pruned Tree", "Random Forest"),
  AUC = c(auc(roc_logit), auc(roc_tree), auc(roc_rf)),
  Accuracy = c(cm_logit$overall["Accuracy"], 
               cm_tree$overall["Accuracy"], 
               cm_rf$overall["Accuracy"])
)
print(comparison_metrics)
##                 Model       AUC  Accuracy
## 1 Logistic Regression 0.8593098 0.8624302
## 2         Pruned Tree 0.9031679 0.9315642
## 3       Random Forest 0.9307560 0.9327863
